## [00:00 - 03:01]

And here is the sales coordinator SOP. So let's go into the scorecard. So it's kind of the scorecard in SOP. Mission of the sales coordinator, coordinating with operations, marketing, and fulfillment of sure sales people are all set up to win. So this scorecard is going to kind of double as an SOP, guys. First thing they're going to be doing is calendar management. Approving and grading applications, that needs done daily. The morning ASAP is literally like, as part of their morning routine, they should be grading your applications before bed. And then also, three to four times batched throughout the day. And then they need to cancel and delegate certain apps and then do the on-the-fence email and borderline app process. They also need to do the sales daily coordinator report and communicate calendar KPIs to marketing incentives daily. So we looked at an example of this already. But here's an example of what it looks like. Yesterday's report, inflow. I think this one was on a Sunday. So we had 13 inbound, 14 total bookings, one outbound. Usually we'd have way more outbound than this, but I think it was a reported on a Monday, which was for a Sunday. Applications, 14. No apps, obviously zero, 14 versus 14. Not claim was zero, meaning all of these outbound apps means the setters claim their own appointments. And then all the closers availability is communicated. So how many booked versus how many available, right? And the KPI for all of these guys is five. So he has five booked, then he's going to be a zero. Four booked, one available. Six booked, obviously a zero because he's over KPI, and so on and so forth. So you can look through that. If you go back through the middle of day process training, I went over this report in a little bit more detail, so you can check that out as well. And then they submit that to Slack daily. They make sure every incoming and appointment has a proper attribution, right? So if it's inbound, is it from YouTube, is it from Facebook? I mean, you can go pretty deep with attribution. So they're in charge of this, depends on how deep you want to go. Outbound, what setter, what source, OK? Pay traffic reporting. So I have my sales coordinator also do all of the paid traffic reporting. So they manually pull paid traffic numbers from the business manager, click phone, active campaign schedule ones, type form, Slack, all of that stuff. I'll show you an example of what our paid traffic support looks like. So we basically have a paid traffic Excel sheet. And then we have a ZAP setup to where once a day just ZAPs the data into our Slack. And that's how I check it. And so we do the last four days, last 10 days, and last 30. And we do it for all these numbers, amount spent, leads, cost for leads, apps, cost for apps, schedule, cost for schedule, live call, cost for call, closes, front end revenue, front end collected, PNL rev, PNL collected, cash row as, total rise. So that's what we do. And yeah, pretty simple. So this is just really doing your paid traffic reporting. Just make sure you don't take this all from Facebook. Like you need to make sure you take the data from the actual source, like the clicks from click funnels,

## [03:01 - 05:33]

the leads from active campaign, the appointments from schedule ones, the applications from type form. That's submitted to Slack daily in the morning as well, based on yesterday's data. Then we do outbound sales reporting as well. So that's going to be pulling numbers from the CRM and whatever call softwares that you're using. So if you see this, sales coordinator stats, we would do this for last seven days, last month to date, and trailing 30. And to be candid, I might switch this towards last seven, or last four, last 10, last 30. So trailing four, trailing 10, trailing 30. It doesn't really matter what you do, but I might as well keep this consistent with what we just did with paid traffic. So then we go with the total leads generated. So how many phone numbers were actually generated? How many dials? How many meaningful conversations or triages, whether that was generated through messenger or outbound? Just how many meaningful conversations that they have? How many calls are actually booked? Total, both inbound and outbound altogether. How many were inbound? How many were outbound? And then through the outbound, what was the attribution? Where did they come from each of the sources? And then the closed clients, what was the total? How many were from outbound? How many from inbound? How much was collected? How much was the revenue? So we do these every single day. And you can obviously do some certain things here. Like outbound dials versus leads is going to give you the frequency of the dial. And you can do percentages based on how qualified they are. But these are the baseline metrics. You want to make sure you get outbound-wise. And then that's submitted to Slack daily. And then they need to make sure salespeople are doing their end of day activities every single day at the beginning of the next day. So before the meeting, they need to make sure all the sales people did their end of day admin checklist from the day before. I would pay this person $3,000 to $4,000. And you're probably not going to want to hire this as a standalone person right away. Like even us right now, we're probably, you know, we have a dozen salespeople. And we're on our way to 1.5 or 1.6 million next month. And honestly, like it's not even a full-time job for us yet. So we have kind of an operations person who does multiple roles. And this is just one of the roles that she does. And she's great. And so usually four grand a month or three grand a month, three to five. And then probably around full-time, you'll need somebody when you're about 10 million to 20 million a year plus. So that's it for this training, guys. Let me know if you have questions on the sales coordinator. We'll see you in the next training.
